questions.text: Two senior team members disagree loudly about the technical approach, and the tension is spreading to the rest of the team. What should the project manager do first?
options: Escalate the disagreement to the project sponsor for a decision
Bring both members together to understand each viewpoint and facilitate a collaborative resolution
Choose the approach favored by the more experienced member to save time
Assign the two members to separate tasks so they no longer interact
questions.explanation: Collaborating/problem-solving addresses the root cause and typically yields the most durable resolution. The PM should facilitate direct dialogue before escalating or forcing a decision.

questions.text: A newly formed team is unsure of roles and looks to the project manager for direction on nearly every task. Which servant-leadership action best fits this stage?
options: Step back and let the team self-organize without guidance
Provide clear direction and remove impediments while the team builds shared norms
Replace team members who ask too many questions
Delay all decisions until the team reaches consensus
questions.explanation: Early (forming) teams need more direction and support. A servant leader provides clarity and removes impediments, gradually shifting to coaching as the team matures.

questions.text: A key stakeholder is consistently unavailable for planned engagement activities. What is the best first step?
options: Remove the stakeholder from the stakeholder register
Proceed without their input to avoid delays
Understand their communication preferences and adjust the engagement approach
Report them to the sponsor as non-cooperative
questions.explanation: Effective stakeholder engagement adapts to each stakeholder's needs and preferences. Understanding why they are unavailable lets the PM tailor the approach rather than exclude them.

questions.text: Midway through execution, the customer requests a change that would expand the deliverable's scope. What should the project manager do first?
options: Implement the change immediately to keep the customer satisfied
Reject the change because the baseline is already approved
Evaluate the change through the integrated change control process
Add the work quietly and absorb it within the current budget
questions.explanation: All changes to a baseline must go through integrated change control so their impact on scope, schedule, cost, and risk is assessed before approval.

questions.text: An activity has an early start of day 4 and a late start of day 9. What is the total float of this activity?
options: 0 days
4 days
5 days
9 days
questions.explanation: Total float = late start − early start = 9 − 4 = 5 days. The activity can slip up to 5 days without delaying the project finish.

questions.text: A project reports EV = 800, PV = 1000, and AC = 900. How is the project performing?
options: Ahead of schedule and under budget
Behind schedule and over budget
On schedule and on budget
Ahead of schedule and over budget
questions.explanation: SV = EV − PV = 800 − 1000 = −200 (behind schedule); CV = EV − AC = 800 − 900 = −100 (over budget). Both variances are negative.

questions.text: New regulations in the industry will affect the project's compliance requirements mid-way through delivery. What should the project manager do?
options: Ignore the regulations until the project is complete
Assess the impact on the project and update plans, risks, and the business case as needed
Cancel the project because requirements changed
Wait for the sponsor to raise the issue before acting
questions.explanation: The PM must monitor the external business environment for compliance and regulatory changes, assess their impact, and adapt the project accordingly to keep it aligned with organizational value.

questions.text: During delivery, the organization's strategic priorities shift and the project's expected benefits are now questionable. What is the best action?
options: Continue as planned because the charter was already approved
Review the business case and benefits with the sponsor to confirm the project is still justified
Immediately terminate the project
Reduce the team size to cut costs
questions.explanation: Projects exist to deliver business value. When strategy shifts, the PM should revisit the business case and benefits realization with the sponsor to confirm continued alignment.

questions.text: A project delivers its scope on time and on budget, but the intended business benefits are not being realized after handover. This is primarily a gap in what?
options: Schedule management
Cost management
Benefits realization and organizational change readiness
Procurement management
questions.explanation: Delivering scope is not the same as realizing value. Sustained benefits depend on benefits realization management and the organization's readiness to adopt the change.

questions.text: You are working at Stratus Innovations, where leadership proposes the delivery of a new customer platform that will impact multiple teams. They need it to be released before October next year, and multiple experts from other systems or teams may be involved. Leadership asks how this initiative should be managed. What will you tell them?
options: Manage it as operations
Manage it as a program
Manage it as a project
Manage it as a portfolio
questions.explanation: Projects are initiated to create value by producing tangible and/or intangible deliverables such as products, services, or other results. Unlike ongoing operations, projects are temporary and have a defined beginning and end. Programs combine multiple projects, and Portfolios combine Programs and Operations. The Standard for Project Management, Page 12, 'Portfolios, Programs, Projects'.

questions.text: Daniel is the project manager at FinTrust Solutions, preparing the business case for a new mobile banking platform. As part of the document he identifies expected outcomes, including increased transaction revenue and improved customer satisfaction and brand reputation. How should Daniel Categorize these benefits in the business case?
options: Both are tangible benefits
Both are intangible benefits
Revenue increase is intangible, while customer satisfaction is tangible.
Revenue increase is tangible, while customer satisfaction is intangible.
questions.explanation: Tangible benefits are benefits that can be measured in financial terms, such as profitability, stockholder equity, and market share. Intangible benefits are benefits that are difficult to measure in financial terms, such as brand recognition, customer satisfaction, and employee well-being. The Standard for Project Management, Page 14, 'Examples of Business Value'.

questions.text: You are the project manager at Secure Bank Corp, implementing a new online banking platform. While developing the project management plan, you identify items like regulations, organizational culture, and IT infrastructure that may influence the project. How should you account for these factors in your project management plan?
options: Document them as enterprise environmental factors.
Treat them as project deliverables
Include them as part of the scope baseline.
Convert them into project risks only.
questions.explanation: Enterprise environmental factors refer to conditions not directly influenced by the project team that impact, constrain or direct the project. The Standard for Project Management, Page 18, 'Enterprise Environmental Factors'.

questions.text: Rose is the project manager at TCS, implementing a new internal reporting system. The PMO required her to use a standard project management plan template and follow established procedures from previous projects. She reviews these templates and guidelines to ensure consistency with organizational practices. How should Rose classify these templates and procedures?
options: Project Deliverables
Enterprise Environmental Factors (EEFs)
Organizational Process Assets (OPAs)
Project Assumptions
questions.explanation: Organizational process assets, depending on the industry, organization and working model, may include the plans, processes, documents, templates, and knowledge repositories specific to and used by the performing organization. These assets influence the management of the project. The Standard for Project Management, Page 19, 'Organizational Process Assets'.

questions.text: You are the project manager at BuildMax Engineering, working on a large infrastructure project. The organization assigns you as the project manager, provides dedicated project resources, and gives you full authority over the project budget and decision-making. What type of organizational structure are you working in?
options: Functional organization
Weak-matrix organization
Project-Oriented organization
Balanced-matrix organization
questions.explanation: Project-oriented organizations are those in which the project manager has a high level of authority and control over the project, including authority over the project team and resources. The Standard for Project Management, Page 22, 'Influences of Organizational Structures on Projects'.

questions.text: Liam works as a manager in the sales department at Prime Manufacturing and would like to start a process improvement initiative. Any project team members are already within his team, but Liam has no additional budget or authority. What type of organizational structure is Liam working in?
options: Project-oriented organization
Functional organization
Balanced-matrix organization
Weak-matrix organization
questions.explanation: Functional organizations are those in which the project is done as part of an existing functional team - there is no designated project coordinator, additional budget or resources. The Standard for Project Management, Page 22, 'Influences of Organizational Structures on Projects'.

questions.text: Sophia is the project manager at Tech Bridge Solutions, leading a system implementation project. During planning, she performs activities such as coordination, collaboration, applying expertise, and providing support for the delivered product. Which function is NOT associated with projects?
options: Providing oversight and coordination
Facilitating and supporting
Applying expertise
Performing ongoing operational support.
questions.explanation: Functions associated with projects include providing oversight and coordination, soliciting and managing feedback, facilitating and supporting, performing work, applying expertise, providing organizational direction and insight, and providing resources. The Standard for Project Management, Pages 24-26, 'Functions Associated with Projects'.

questions.text: An employee in the organization secures funding for the project, provides strategic direction, and helps remove organizational obstacles to ensure project success. What role is this person performing in the project?
options: Product Owner
Project Sponsor
Project Customer
Project Manager
questions.explanation: A sponsor is an individual or a group that provides resources and support for the portfolio, program, or project, and is accountable for enabling success. A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, 8th Ed. Page 274, 'Sponsor'.

questions.text: Roy is part of the project management team at Transglobal Logistics, working on a system upgrade project. The team collaborates with stakeholders and managers to align with organizational objectives and secure resources, while other activities are also being considered. Which of the following is something the project management team would do?
options: Provide duration and cost estimates on the deliverables.
Coordinate with functional managers for demands on critical resources.
Verify that deliverables meet their quality requirements.
Approve the final product on behalf of the customer
questions.explanation: The project management team proactively interacts and negotiates with other project management teams, portfolio managers, project managers, and functional managers for demands on critical resources, priorities on project funding, receipt or distribution of deliverables, and in aligning project goals and objectives with those of the organization. The Standard for Project Management, Page 29, 'Interaction Between the Project Management Team and Project Aspects'.

questions.text: You are working with a project sponsor in the early stages of a cloud platform implementation at CSI. As you work with the project sponsor to put together the business case, they ask when the project will begin delivering value. What will you tell them?
options: The project value will be realized after the project is fully complete.
The project benefits can be realized during project execution phases.
Project value will be realized once benefits are formally measured.
Value can be realized throughout the project, at the end of the project or following project completion.
questions.explanation: Value can be realized throughout the project, at the end of the project, or following project completion. The Standard for Project Management, page 41, 'Focus on value'.

questions.text: You are a project manager working on a chip fabrication project at NG Micro Devices. A functional manager raises concerns about the quality of deliverables being passed to their department and want assurance that standards will be consistently met. How will you manage this on your project?
options: Quality should be verified through final inspection before delivery.
Quality should be integrated into processes to consistently achieve target quality thresholds.
Quality should be managed separately from project processes to ensure independence.
Quality should focus on correcting defects after they are identified.
questions.explanation: Embed quality into processes and deliverables to maintain a consistent focus on achieving target quality thresholds. This emphasis on quality helps to ensure outcomes that meet project objectives and align with the needs, requirements, and acceptance criteria set by relevant stakeholders. The Standard for Project Management, Page 43, 'Embed Quality Into Processes and Deliverables'.

questions.text: Rafael is managing a government megaproject at SGI involving multiple vendors. A conflict arises between vendor teams over shift rotations, creating tension and affecting productivity. Now the issue is starting to affect project deliverables. How should Rafael address this situation?
options: Force the vendors to follow the agreed contract terms
Let the vendors resolve the issue on their own
Facilitate discussions to identify root causes and agree on a solution
Assign your best team member to manage the conflict
questions.explanation: The collaborative approach not only resolves the conflict but also removes friction that could undermine quality and productivity, fostering a more cohesive and motivated project team. The Standard for Project Management, Page 48, 'Be an Accountable Leader: Principle in Action'.

questions.text: You are in the planning phase of a refinery expansion project at PetraCore Energy Ld. An executive manager raises concerns about increasing staffing costs and several identified risks early in the project. What will you tell them about the cost and risk on the project?
options: Cost and staffing are highest at the beginning, while risk and uncertainty increase toward project completion.
Cost and staffing gradually decrease throughout the project, while risk and uncertainty remain constant.
Cost and staffing increase as work progresses and drop near completion, while risk and uncertainty decrease over time.
Cost and staffing remain stable throughout the project, while risk and uncertainty fluctuate unpredictably.
questions.explanation: In many predictive project scenarios, cost and staffing levels are low at the start, increase as work is carried out, and then drop rapidly as the project or phase comes to an end. Regardless of the project life cycle, risk and uncertainty are typically highest at the start of the project or phase and decrease over time as decisions are made and deliverables are accepted. The Standard for Project Management, Page 59, 'Impact of Variables Over Time'.

questions.text: Dr. Mitchell is managing a drug development project at EB Inc. The project operates in a highly regulated environment with phase gate approvals. Scope, schedule, cost, and risks are defined early, and strict change control with extensive upfront planning is required. Which development approach is being used?
options: Adaptive approach
Hybrid approach
Incremental approach
Predictive approach
questions.explanation: A predictive approach may be optimal when the project scope can be stabilized early in the project. A predictive approach may also be referred to as a waterfall, plan-driven, or traditional approach. The scope, schedule, cost, resource needs, quality requirements, and risks can be well defined in the early phases of the project life cycle and are expected to remain relatively stable. The Standard for Project Management, Page 61, 'Predictive Approaches'.

questions.text: Jason is leading a mobile application project at Blue Peak Digital Solutions. The team works in short iterations, delivering features regularly and refining their approach based on feedback. One person is responsible for the approved scope and its value to the organization. Which development approach is being used?
options: Adaptive approach
Predictive approach
Hybrid approach
Incremental approach
questions.explanation: Adaptive approaches, also referred to as change-driven or agile approaches, are useful when a project's requirements and technical solution are highly uncertain, volatile, and likely to change. A clear vision is established early, and known requirements are progressively elaborated, changed or replaced based on stakeholder feedback, the environment, or unexpected events. The Standard for Project Management, Page 63, 'Adaptive Approaches'.

questions.text: Ethan is working on a project at West Gate Group that will deliver a new enterprise platform. The software component has constantly evolving requirements, while the data center infrastructure is built using upfront planning and a formal change control approach, with the two brought together in a final Sprint at the end. Which development approach should Ethan use?
options: Predictive approach
Hybrid approach
Incremental approach
Adaptive approach
questions.explanation: A Hybrid development approach combines adaptive and predictive approaches, incorporating elements from both. For example, a project may have two main deliverables, where one is developed using an adaptive approach and the other using a predictive approach. The Standard for Project Management, Page 65, 'Hybrid Approaches'.

questions.text: You have just taken over as project manager for the Orion CRM upgrade project, where many stakeholders are not available, others are disengaged and the success criteria is unclear. This is an example of:
options: Project constraints
Lagging indicators
Leading indicators
Issues to be managed
questions.explanation: Leading indicators indicate upcoming changes or reveal trends in the project. The lack of a risk management process, stakeholders who are not available or engaged, or poorly defined project success criteria are all examples of leading indicators that project performance may be at risk. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Page 14, 'Leading Indicators'.

questions.text: You have been assigned to lead a new patient record system project at CHS. The business case has been approved, and key stakeholders are identified. Senior leadership wants the project to start immediately and without delay. What will you do next?
options: Develop the project management plan to define how the project will be executed
Perform a kick-off meeting with the project team
Work with the project sponsor to approve the project charter
Perform a make-or-buy analysis to determine the resources to be used.
questions.explanation: The Initiate Project or Phase process officially authorizes the start of a project and grants the project manager the authority to allocate organizational resources to project activities by creating a project charter or similar document. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Page 17, 'Initiate Project or Phase'.

questions.text: You have been hired to manage a retail expansion initiative. As planning begins, the cost of some materials rises significantly. Senior stakeholders raise concerns about the cost versus the delivered value of the project, and whether it will be worthwhile. What will you do next?
options: Review the Project Management Plan
Review the Financial Management Plan
Review the Project Charter
Review the Business Case
questions.explanation: A business case is a documented economic feasibility study used to establish validity of the benefits to be delivered by a portfolio component, program or project. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Page 17, 'Initiate Project or Phase' and Page 116, 'Business Case'.

questions.text: Elijah is initiating a data center expansion project at QGS. Early planning relies on uncertain factors such as utility availability and the accuracy of the vendor timelines. As Elijah documents these initial estimates for cost and duration, what will he NOT do?
options: Add the factors to the Assumption Log
Add the factors to the Risk Register
Add the factors to the Basis of Estimates
Add the factors to the Issue Log
questions.explanation: An assumption log records all project assumptions and constraints throughout the life cycle, including updates and status changes. High-level constraints originate in the business case and flow into the charter, while lower-level assumptions are identified during planning and execution activities. The Risk Register records uncertainties. The Issue Log records impacts to the project that have occurred. PMBOK Guide 8th Ed, Page 17, 'Initiate Project or Phase', and Page 114 'Assumption Log'.

questions.text: You are managing a large infrastructure project at Granite Works Engineering. You have conducted a kick-off meeting with the project team and multiple team leads have provided their high-level strategies. You want to put these in a document that you can change and configure over time. What will you do next?
options: Create the configuration management plan
Create the project management plan
Create the project charter
Create the change management plan
questions.explanation: The project management plan specifies how the project will be executed, monitored, controlled and closed. Its content varies depending on the project's application context and complexity. The plan can be either high level or detailed, tailored to the project's specific needs. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Pages 18-19, 'Integrate and Align Project Plans'.

questions.text: A project team at Redwood Industrial Systems is preparing to acquire specialized component. They are defining evaluation criteria, contract types, and deciding whether to build internally or outsource the work. No vendors have been contacted yet. What should the project manager do next?
options: Issue procurement documents and request proposals from vendors
Evaluate vendor proposals and select the best supplier
Develop the sourcing strategy including make-or-buy decisions
Advertise the opportunity to gather additional vendors.
questions.explanation: The Plan Sourcing Strategy process entails documenting project sourcing decisions, specifying the source selection approach, determining the scope of work for external sourcing, and selecting the appropriate contracts and sources for delivering the work. These include make-or-buy decisions. PMBOK Guide 8th Ed. Pages 19-20, 'Plan Sourcing Strategy'.

questions.text: A project requires outsourcing specialized engineering work. Multiple vendors have expressed their interest in submitting a proposal, but each have different ideas on what the project is about. What should the project manager do?
options: Conduct a bidder conference
Use advertising for the proposal
Perform a make-or-buy analysis
Clarify the source selection criteria
questions.explanation: A bidder conference ensures all prospective sellers receive the same information and clarifications, promoting fairness and a shared understanding of requirements without giving any vendor an advantage. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Pages 19-20, 'Plan Sourcing Strategy' and Page 265 'Bidder Conference'.

questions.text: Irene is preparing a large infrastructure project at West Core Development. Different teams propose conflicting approaches for acquiring materials and services, causing delays in procurement planning. Irene needs to align the overall approach, including contract types and supplier selection methods. What should she do?
options: Develop the statement of work
Perform the make-or-buy analysis
Develop the project bid documents.
Develop a sourcing strategy plan
questions.explanation: Plan Sourcing Strategy documents sourcing decisions, defines the source selection approach, and determines work for external sourcing. The sourcing strategy plan describes what will be insourced or outsourced, including rationale, selection criteria, and considerations such as value, schedule, risk, and financial impact. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Page 20 'Plan Sourcing Strategy' and page 141 'Sourcing strategy plan'.

questions.text: Rick is planning a manufacturing project at Trade Corp. A critical component can be produced internally or sourced from a specialized supplier. There is pressure from internal stakeholders to begin work as quickly as possible, and there is limited budget for additional items. What will Rick do next?
options: Develop the procurement management plan
Conduct a cost-benefit analysis
Perform a make-or-buy analysis
Issue requests for proposal (RFP)
questions.explanation: Make-or-buy analysis is a decision-making process that gathers and analyzes product requirements against alternatives, including internal production or external purchase. It determines whether project work should be performed by the team or acquired from outside sources based on the best option. PMBOK Guide 8th Ed. Pages 19-20 'Plan Sourcing Strategy' and page 180 'Make-or-buy analysis'.

questions.text: Aiden is managing a facilities upgrade at CM Holdings. The project requires standard electrical components with clearly defined specifications, and multiple suppliers are available at different price points causing confusion and delays in the selection process. What should he do?
options: Conduct a bidder conference
Issue a Request For Proposal
Issue a Request For Information
Issue a Request For Quote
questions.explanation: A request for quote (RFQ) is used when price is the main deciding factor and the proposed solution is readily available. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Page 20, 'Plan Sourcing Strategy', and page 248 'Request for quote'.

questions.text: Rina is executing a warehouse automation project at Grid Line Logistics. Several modifications have been requested throughout the project, and now there is confusion about what should be implemented and in what sequence. What will Rina do next?
options: Review the Scope Statement
Review the Change Log
Review the Issue Log
Review the Risk Register
questions.explanation: Manage Project Execution involves leading and performing work defined in the plan and implementing approved changes to meet objectives, ensuring deliverables meet scope requirements. A change log records all submitted changes and their status, documenting the disposition of change requests throughout the project. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Page 22, 'Manage Project Execution' and page 116 'Change log'.

questions.text: You are managing a production system upgrade at Iron Works Manufacturing. During execution, your team is delivering outputs, implementing approved changes, and tracking performance data. A stakeholder raises concerns about the consistency and reliability of the outputs being produced. What should you do?
options: Reassess the scope and quality plans to improve output consistency
Ensure deliverables are produced according to the defined scope baseline
Raise these concerns with the steering committee
Complete a full analysis of the quality risks being raised
questions.explanation: The Manage Project Execution process involves leading and performing the work defined in the project management plan and implementing approved changes to meet the project's objectives. At this point, quality should be focused on ensuring that deliverables are being produced according to the agreed-upon requirements set in the scope baseline. PMBOK Guide 8th Ed. Pages 22-23, 'Manage Project Execution'.

questions.text: A project team at CD Solutions is developing a customer analytics platform. Midway through execution, a security officer reports unauthorized access to sensitive project data. This could cause issues with the deliverables in the future. What should the project manager do first?
options: Note it in the Issue Log
Note it in the risk register
Brainstorm ways to handle the security breach with the team
Escalate the breach to senior managers within the organization
questions.explanation: When a problem has already occurred, it is treated as an issue. The issue log is used to document, track and manage issues before further action is taken. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Pages 22-23, 'Manage Project Execution' and page 123 'Issue Log'.

questions.text: Omar is managing a pharmaceutical packaging project at NCL. Quality inspections repeatedly identify defects caused by inconsistent procedures across shifts, leading to rework and delays. Omar wants to address the underlying cause and improve consistency moving forward. What should Omar do next?
options: Update the team's work instructions
Implement check sheets
Assign a quality lead
Apply process analysis & improvement
questions.explanation: Manage Quality Assurance implements planned and systematic activities from the quality management plan to ensure outputs meet requirements. Process improvement focuses on analyzing and enhancing processes to increase efficiency, quality, and value by identifying improvements, reducing waste, and achieving better performance and customer satisfaction. PMBOK Guide 8th Ed. Page 23 'Manage Quality Assurance' and page 188 'Process Improvement'.

questions.text: You are managing a production system project at Forge Line Industries. During execution, inconsistencies are observed in how teams follow procedures and organizational standards. Leadership want to identify gaps in processes and improve overall performance. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Increase inspections to find defects in completed deliverables
Re-baseline the project to address process inconsistencies
Conduct additional testing to validate product performance
Perform audits to identify improvement areas
questions.explanation: The Manage Quality Assurance process implements a set of planned and systematic acts and processes defined within the project's quality management plan. This can help build confidence that a future output will be completed in a manner that meets the specified requirements and expectations through quality assurance tools and techniques such as quality audits and failure analysis. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Pages 23-24, 'Manage Quality Assurance'.

questions.text: A project team is missing deadlines due to inconsistent task execution. Junior members are completing extra, unnecessary work, while senior members report inefficiencies in the way items are delivered, with defects occurring as a result. What should the project manager do?
options: Ask the team to swarm around the issue and solve it.
Implement a general training program
Work with the team to create checklists
Reassign tasks to more experienced team members
questions.explanation: A checklist is a list of items or actions used as reminders, developed from historical information and lessons learned from similar projects to identify potential risks. Collaborating with the team ensures alignment and improves performance without immediate escalation or major restructuring. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Pages 23-24 'Manage Quality Assurance' and page 151 'Checklists'.

questions.text: You are managing a digital banking platform upgrade at Southland Financial Group. Midway through execution, conflict arises between developers and compliance officers over security requirements. You facilitate a session with the team and find a solution to move forward. What will you do with this new information?
options: Update the risk register with the identified risks and mitigation strategies
Document and maintain a lessons learned register throughout the project
Ensure all project knowledge is archived into organizational process assets at project closure
Develop a knowledge management plan to formalize information sharing
questions.explanation: Manage Project Knowledge is the process of using existing knowledge and creating new knowledge to achieve project objectives and support organizational learning. A lessons learned register records knowledge gained during a project, phase, or iteration to improve future performance for the team and organization. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Pages 24-26 'Manage Project Knowledge' and page 123 'Lessons learned register'.

questions.text: You are managing a data migration project at VLM. After completing a critical migration phase, discrepancies are discovered between expected and actual results, leading to rework and missed timelines. What should you do next?
options: Conduct an AAR with the team
Conduct a Risk Workshop with the team
Conduct a SWOT analysis with the team
Adjust the schedule and resource assignments
questions.explanation: After-action reviews are a structured process used to analyze what happened, why it happened, and how it can be improved. They help enhance team performance by reflection on actions and outcomes and are conducted after projects or major milestones to capture lessons learned and inform future work. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Pages 24-26, 'Manage Project Knowledge', and page 145, 'After-action Reviews'.

questions.text: James is managing a commercial build at Evercrest Corp. During execution he reviews cost and schedule data and notices the project is going off track. Stakeholders are starting to notice and want an update on what's going on. What should James do first to respond to this situation?
options: Implement corrective actions to bring performance back on track
Update the project management plan to reflect the changes in cost and schedule
Analyze performance data and turn it into work performance information
Escalate the issue to the project management team to avoid further delays.
questions.explanation: Monitor and Control Project Performance is the process of tracking, reviewing and reporting the overall project progress to meet the performance objectives defined in the project management plan. Work performance data is compared to create work performance information which is ultimately sent as a report to stakeholders. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Pages 26-27, 'Monitor and Control Project Performance'.

questions.text: A project team is repeatedly submitting deliverables late for internal quality checks, despite clear expectations being defined earlier. This pattern is beginning to affect overall timelines. The project manager wants to prevent further occurrences. What should the project manager do?
options: Raise a change request for the affected items
Perform Ishikawa analysis
Review the quality management plan with the team
Initiate an internal audit
questions.explanation: An Ishikawa diagram is root cause analysis, which helps identify the underlying reason for recurring issues, enabling the project manager to address the source of the problem and prevent future occurrences. PMBOK Guide 8th Ed. Page 27, 'Monitor and Control Project Performance' and page 150, 'Cause and Effect diagram'.

questions.text: Leah is managing a highway expansion project at Granite Link Infrastructure. After baselines are approved, a stakeholder proposes a design change that may impact cost and schedule. The team is ready to implement it immediately to avoid delays. How should Leah handle this situation?
options: Submit a formal change request and assess its impacts
Review the benefits and proceed with implementation
Defer the change until sufficient performance data is available to reassess it
Update project plans to reflect the proposed change and communicate it to stakeholders
questions.explanation: Once the project has a baseline, all changes should go through a formal process to assess and implement changes. Change requests should typically include information on the estimated schedule and cost impacts before they can be approved. PMBOK Guide 8th Ed. Pages 28-29, 'Assess and Implement Changes'.

questions.text: A project at EI Solutions is in progress when several change requests are raised simultaneously. One of your developers is implementing their change immediately, while someone from the business is holding stakeholder meetings to review the project impact of their change. You are finding it hard to keep track. What will you do next?
options: Review the change management plan with the team
Get a recommendation from the Change Control Board
Review and update the Change Log
Approve the most critical changes immediately
questions.explanation: Assess and Implement Changes occurs throughout the project, as changes may be proposed by any stakeholder and affect scope, plans, or documents. The change management plan defines how changes are managed, establishes the change control board, and outlines roles, authority and implementation of the change control system. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Pages 28-29 'Assess and Implement Changes' and page 116 'Change Management Plan'.

questions.text: Marina is managing a power grid stabilization project at Grid Core Energy. A critical update must be implemented immediately to prevent system failure, but the team is divided on the best approach and time is limited. Marina needs to act quickly to move forward. What should Marina do next?
options: Facilitate group decision-making on the best approach
Apply majority voting on the different options
Use multi-criteria decision analysis
Use autocratic decision-making
questions.explanation: Autocratic decision-making is appropriate when time is critical and a quick decision is required, allowing the project manager to act decisively without waiting for consensus. PMBOK Guide 8th Ed. Page 29, 'Assess and Implement Changes' and page 163 'Autocratic decision-making'.

questions.text: Olivia is managing a highway expansion project at WIC using a predictive approach. As the project nears completion, stakeholders push to release resources quickly. Olivia wants to ensure proper administrative closure while meeting all project objectives. What should she NOT do next?
options: Confirm all deliverables are completed, formally accepted, and closure criteria are satisfied
Verify that all costs have been charged to the project
Reassign or release project personnel
Ensure all documents and deliverables are updated and all issues are resolved
questions.explanation: For project closure, the project manager ensures all work is completed and objectives are met by reviewing the project management plan, updating documents and deliverables, and resolving issues. Resources should not be released until all closure activities and exit criteria are confirmed as completed. PMBOK Guide 8th Ed. Pages 31-32, 'Close Project or Phase'.

questions.text: At Northbridge Utilities Group, a project component has recently been handed over to operations. However, team members are still being pulled back to support ongoing issues. The project manager wants to confirm what activities and items were completed during the project handover. What will she do next?
options: Review the procurement agreements and organizational process assets updates
Review the quality report and lessons learned register
Review the final report and scope management plan
Review the final report and organizational process assets updates
questions.explanation: During project closure, the project manager ensures all work is completed and documented. The final report, updated documents, and organizational process assets confirm closure. The final report summarizes performance, including scope, quality, cost, schedule results, benefits achieved, and how risks and issues were addressed. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Pages 31-33, 'Close Project or Phase' and page 121, 'Close Project or Phase'.

questions.text: You are a project manager at SBC, preparing for a commercial building project. Before defining detailed requirements, leadership wants the team to follow a consistent approach to ensure only value-adding work is performed. What should be the next step?
options: Begin creating the work breakdown structure to define all project work
Develop the scope management plan
Collect detailed, value requirements from stakeholders
Validate scope with stakeholders to confirm acceptance of deliverables
questions.explanation: The objective of Plan Scope Management is to create a scope management plan. This process defines how the project will be delivered by establishing all the work required to complete the project and eliminating or removing unnecessary work that will not add value to the project. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Page 40, 'Plan Scope Management'.

questions.text: A new mobile app is being initiated at Crestview Digital. Early discussion reveal differing stakeholder expectations about features and functionality. As the project manager, you are asked how the team will gather requirements to ensure the solution delivers value to the organization. What should you do?
options: Develop a requirements traceability matrix to trace requirements to their scope
Conduct focus groups and benchmarking to collect and compare stakeholder requirements
Create a scope baseline to define approved deliverables and boundaries
Perform quality audits to ensure requirements meet organizational standards.
questions.explanation: The objective of Elicit and Analyze Requirements is to define and document stakeholders' needs for features and functions to ensure quality and value delivery. Business requirements outline strategic objectives and high-level needs of the organization, ensuring alignment with broader organizational goals. Benchmarking is used as a technique. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Pages 40-41, 'Elicit and Analyze Requirements'.

questions.text: Two parallel project streams at NT industries are being combined into a single initiative. One uses iterative practices, while the other follows a detailed planning approach. After integration, there is confusion about the varied stakeholder requirements and how the team will deliver them as project scope. What should the project manager do?
options: Create a requirements traceability matrix
Integrate all requirements into a single product backlog
Develop a new work breakdown structure
Update the Requirements Management Plan
questions.explanation: A requirements traceability matrix provides a structured way to track requirements from multiple sources, ensuring alignment, visibility and completeness across the project. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Pages 40-41 'Elicit and Analyze Requirements' and pages 131-132 'Requirements traceability matrix'.

questions.text: You are working with your team to gather requirements that define the organization's overall goals and intended outcomes for a new initiative. These requirements will help ensure alignment with long-term objectives. What type of requirements are these?
options: Solution requirements
Stakeholder requirements
Business requirements
Transition requirements
questions.explanation: The objective of Elicit and Analyze Requirements is to define and document stakeholders' needs for features and functions to ensure quality and value delivery. Business requirements outline strategic objectives and high-level organizational needs, ensuring alignment with broader organizational goals. PMBOK Guide 8th Ed. Pages 40-41, 'Elicit and Analyze Requirements', and page 130, 'Business requirements'.

questions.text: You are managing a project for a new wearable device at Fitness Plus. Users report inconsistent experiences in the device, but the root cause is unclear. The team has several competing ideas for improvements, and stakeholders want a solution that truly addresses user needs rather than assumptions. What should the project manager do next?
options: Use Affinity Diagramming with the team
Use Kano Analysis with the team
Use Design Thinking with the team
Use Pareto Analysis with the team
questions.explanation: Prototyping and design thinking emphasize understanding user needs through iterative testing and feedback, ensuring solutions are validated in real conditions before full deployment. PMBOK Guide 8th Ed. Pages 40-41, 'Elicit and Analyze Requirements' and page 168 'Design Thinking'.

questions.text: Stakeholders are providing conflicting inputs at VDS during requirements discussions, making it difficult to prioritize features. Meetings are being dominated by the highest paid and loudest voices in the room leading to some missed requirements, and time is running out before delivery begins. What should the project manager do?
options: Facilitate an open brainstorming session
Conduct individual interviews
Lead and facilitate a focus group
Use the nominal group technique
questions.explanation: The nominal group technique is a structured method for group decision-making that gathers input systematically. It addresses unproductive discussions by equalizing participation, minimizing dominance of vocal members, and ensuring all ideas are considered before discussion, especially in large groups handling controversial topics. PMBOK Guide 8th Ed. Pages 40-41, 'Elicit and Analyze Requirements' and page 184, 'Nominal group'.

questions.text: Jane is gathering requirements for a customer portal at BLS. The team are working well together but have only generated a handful of ideas so far. The project sponsor would like to speed things up. What should Jane do next?
options: Use an affinity diagram
Use brainstorming
Conduct interviews with senior users
Use the nominal group technique
questions.explanation: Brainstorming is a technique used in group settings to generate ideas quickly, led by a facilitator. It includes idea generation and analysis, and is used to gather data, solutions or ideas from stakeholders, subject-matter experts, and team members. PMBOK Guide 8th Ed. Page 41 'Elicit and Analyze Requirements' and page 149, 'Brainstorming'.

questions.text: Sam is managing feature delivery for a mobile banking app at Crest View Digital. Only one feature can be added to the next sprint. Each option has a different value potential and development time. Sam wants to maximize value delivered within the sprint. Which feature should be selected?
options: Feature A: $12,000 per week, 4 weeks duration
Feature B: $16,000 per week, 2 weeks duration
Feature C: $10,000 per week, 3 weeks duration
Feature D: $14,000 per week, 5 weeks duration
questions.explanation:
Requirements can be prioritized based on methods such as MoSCoW, 100-point methods, cost of delay or Kano analysis. The Cost of Delay is calculated as (value potential / duration to deliver):
A = 3,000
B = 8,000
C = 3,333
D = 2,800
Feature B provides the highest value relative to time and should be prioritized. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Page 41, 'Elicit and Analyze Requirements' and page 187, 'Prioritization/Ranking'.

questions.text: A project has been launched at HR Group to improve customer satisfaction within a short timeframe. During planning, the team struggles to identify ways to improve the product. The Project Sponsor would like proven ideas based on what has worked well in similar projects or organizations. What should the project manager do next?
options: Use benchmarking with the team
Use analogous estimating with the team
Use focus groups with the team
Review the product with the senior developer
questions.explanation: Benchmarking compares actual or planned products, processes and practices with those of comparable organizations to identify good practices and improvement opportunities. It may involve internal or external projects across different industries, allowing analogies and providing a basis for measuring performance. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Page 41, 'Elicit and Analyze Requirements' and page 149, 'Benchmarking'.

questions.text: Jonah is managing a product development project at RLM. The team needs to clearly organize all deliverables and decompose the work into manageable components to support planning and execution. What should Jonah do next?
options: Organize deliverables into a work breakdown structure
Capture requirements as user stories and refine them iteratively
Prioritize features in a backlog for incremental delivery
Validate the completed deliverables with stakeholders
questions.explanation: The objective of Define Scope is to develop a detailed or high-level description of the project, product, and value expected to be delivered, as well as a quality management plan. In predictive approaches, these deliverables should be structured in the WBS. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Pages 41-42, 'Define Scope'.

questions.text: Nate is managing a manufacturing facility upgrade using a predictive approach. Nate has elicited the requirements from project stakeholders and turned them into project scope. Nate wants to add more details to the work packages to ensure clarity and alignment across the team. What will he do next?
options: Create a responsibility assignment matrix (RAM) to define roles and responsibilities
Perform quality control and verify the work packages
Develop a clear requirements traceability matrix
Develop a detailed WBS dictionary
questions.explanation: The WBS represents a structured, hierarchical decomposition of the total scope of work into smaller, manageable work packages that can be easily assigned, tracked, and measured, ensuring that all stakeholders have a clear and shared understanding of the project deliverables. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Page 42, 'Develop Scope Structure'.

questions.text: At Redwood Biologics, Diane is managing a pharmaceutical product development project. During monitoring, inconsistencies in deliverable quality are identified. Stakeholders request additional information on these issues to support improvements. What should Diane produce?
options: Work performance Data
The scope baseline
Change requests in the change log
The quality report
questions.explanation: Monitor and Control Scope is the ongoing process of monitoring and managing changes to project scope, and measuring deliverable quality and value. It includes recording results to assess performance. A quality report documents quality issues, corrective action recommendations, and findings from quality control activities. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Page 42, 'Monitor and Control Scope', and pages 129-130 'Quality Reports'.

questions.text: Marcus is overseeing a bridge construction project at BEL. Several completed components have passed internal quality checks. The client is now reviewing these outputs against agreed criteria to determine formal approval. What should Marcus obtain as an input to help the client with their decision?
options: Work performance reports summarizing project progress and quality metrics
Validated deliverables
Verified deliverables
Change requests addressing gaps identified during stakeholder review.
questions.explanation: The Validate Scope process checks processes used to achieve quality standards and formalizes acceptance of deliverables, ensuring value and quality. Verified deliverables are completed outputs confirmed for correctness through Monitor and Control Scope, which determines whether deliverables meet defined requirements. PMBOK Guide 8th Ed. Page 44, 'Validate Scope' and page 143 'Verified deliverables'.

questions.text: Priya has just taken over a nationwide fiber network project at HT Solutions, where leadership expresses their concern about how schedule decisions are being made and controlled throughout the project. They want to provide clear guidance and direction for coordinating schedule activities. What should Priya do next?
options: Develop the project schedule to define activity durations and sequencing
Establish a schedule baseline to measure performance against planned timelines
Develop a resource management plan to align staffing with schedule needs
Create a schedule management plan and baseline this with the team
questions.explanation: Plan Schedule Management establishes policies, procedures, and documentation for developing, managing and maintaining the schedule, providing guidance on how it will be managed. The schedule management plan defines criteria and activities for developing, monitoring, and controlling the project schedule. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Page 39, 'Plan Schedule Management' and 139 'Schedule management plan'.

questions.text: You are managing a project team at OP Security, where leaders in the business would like to see your plan for managing the schedule. What do you need to know before creating your schedule management plan?
options: The sequence of project deliverables
Estimates on project activities from the team
The development approach
Methods to be used for schedule compression
questions.explanation: Plan Schedule Management establishes policies, procedures, and documentation for managing the schedule, providing guidance throughout the project. The development approach is an input to this and will impact how we manage the schedule. PMBOK Guide 8th Ed. Page 49, 'Plan Schedule Management', and page 139 'Schedule management plan'.

questions.text: Noah is managing a core banking system upgrade at Granite Peak Financial. After multiple iterations analyzing the sequence, durations, and resources, stakeholders approve the final schedule. What will Noah do next?
options: Proceed with project delivery
Baseline the schedule
Review the impact of schedule sequence on project resources
Add the schedule to the project status report
questions.explanation: Develop Schedule is the process of analyzing sequences, durations, resource requirements, and constraints to create a schedule model for execution, monitoring, and controlling. It is iterative. The schedule baseline is the output of this process, and is the approved version used for comparison and changed only through formal change control procedures. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Page 49, 'Develop Schedule', and page 138 'Schedule baseline'.

questions.text: A project at FI Group shows several critical activities requiring the same specialized engineer at overlapping times. This is creating unrealistic workload expectations and risking delays in the future. What should the project manager do?
options: Apply resource leveling
Apply schedule fast tracking
Raise it as a risk and brainstorm responses with your team
Apply resource smoothing
questions.explanation: Resource leveling adjusts the project schedule to address resource constraints, ensuring resources are not overallocated and that work is scheduled according to actual availability. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Page 50, 'Develop Schedule' and page 193, 'Resource leveling'.

questions.text: While planning a product launch at Nexora Labs, the team struggles to finalize the schedule. Near-term requirements are clear, but work planned for later is uncertain due to evolving requirements. There is pressure from the project sponsor to begin work quickly. What should the project manager do?
options: Immediately estimate activity durations
Develop the project schedule for approval
Use rolling wave planning with the team
Raise the future uncertainty as a risk
questions.explanation: Develop Schedule analyzes sequences, durations, resources, and constraints to create a schedule model for execution and control. It is iterative and may require revising estimates to establish a baseline. Rolling wave planning progressively elaborates near-term work in detail while future work remains at a higher level. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Pages 49-50, 'Develop Schedule' and page 196 'Rolling wave planning'.

questions.text: Reina is managing a new product launch at AC Goods. Packaging design must be finalized before mass production, but labeling can begin once initial drafts are approved. Additionally, quality inspection must wait two days after production finishes. What should Reina do next?
options: Use fast tracking
Apply leads and lags
Apply schedule crashing
Update activity duration estimates
questions.explanation: Leads and lags adjust the timing of successor activities relative to predecessors, allowing earlier starts or intentional delays within defined dependency relationships. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Page 50, 'Develop Schedule', and pages 179-180, 'Leads and Lags'.

questions.text: Elena is managing a warehouse automation project at SLG. As she monitors the project, she reviews the schedule performance and sees some differences that may require action, with a basis for interpreting the current results. What will Elena use to make the required decisions?
options: Work performance data
Work performance reports
Work performance information
The schedule basis of estimates
questions.explanation: Monitor and Control Schedule is the process of monitoring project status to update the schedule and manage changes based on actual or forecasted performance, maintaining a realistic schedule. Work performance information is analyzed data compared with the project management plan, project documents, and other performance details. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Page 53, 'Monitor and Control Schedule', and page 144, 'Work performance information'.

questions.text: At ZP Logistics, Aaron is overseeing a complex project with multiple parallel activities competing for limited scheduling options. As delays begin to emerge, different sequencing scenarios are proposed, each with trade-offs in duration and resource use. The team cannot agree on the best path forward. How should he proceed?
options: Apply a branch and bound technique
Focus on compressing the schedule using fast tracking
Re-sequence activities based on team consensus
Add buffers to schedule estimates
questions.explanation: Monitor and Control Schedule tracks project status, updates the schedule, and manages changes based on actual or forecasted performance to maintain a realistic schedule. The branch and bound technique solves optimization problems by exploring and pruning subproblems to identify an optimal schedule that minimizes completion time. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Pages 53-54, 'Monitor and Control Schedule', and page 150, 'Branch and Bound'.

questions.text: Lloyd is managing a hospital construction project at CPB. The completion date has been moved earlier, but the budget cannot be increased. Several activities could overlap, though this introduces some risk. Lloyd needs to shorten the timeline while staying within constraints. Which approach should he apply?
options: Re-sequence tasks
Adjust activity estimates
Apply schedule crashing
Use schedule fast tracking
questions.explanation: Schedule compression shortens schedule duration without reducing scope to meet constraints. Techniques include crashing, which adds resources at increased cost, and fast-tracking which overlaps activities previously planned in sequence. Both focus on the critical path and may increase risk, cost or rework while addressing negative float conditions. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Page 34, 'Monitor and Control Schedule' and pages 196-197 'Schedule compression'.

questions.text: A new investment platform project is being initiated at KC Holdings. During early planning discussions, the team raises questions about how financial activities will be handled, including acceptable estimate ranges and triggers for escalation. What will the project manager show them?
options: The cost basis of estimates
The configuration management plan
The financial management plan
The risk management plan with triggers and response strategies
questions.explanation: Financial Management Plan defines how project revenues and expenses will be estimated, budgeted, managed, monitored, and controlled. The financial management plan is a component of the project management plan that describes how costs will be planned, structured, and controlled. PMBOK Guide 8th Ed. Page 62, 'Plan Financial Management' and pages 121-122, 'Financial management plan'.

questions.text: Maya, a project manager at Orion Axis Consulting, is working on a project to reduce carbon dioxide in the air through Direct Air Capture, with significant interest from large organizations who would benefit from these strategies. As Maya finalizes the plan to manage finances on the project, what should she do next?
options: Note NGOs in the Funding Strategy
Send the Financial Management Plan to the Sponsor for approval
Review the Project Charter for the high-level cost
Engage experts in the business on the costs involved
questions.explanation: Plan Financial Management defines how project revenues and expenses will be estimated, budgeted, managed, monitored, and controlled, providing guidance on financial management. Funding Strategy is an output of Plan Financial Management. Non-governmental organizations (NGOs) are funding this project. PMBOK Guide 8th Ed. Pages 62-63, 'Plan Financial Management' and pages 122-123, 'Funding Strategy'.

questions.text: While planning at HN Manufacturing, Diego, the project manager, is in the early stage of a new project and needs a quick estimate to support initial planning discussions. There is limited detailed information available but a senior team member mentions having worked on a similar project in the past. What should Diego do next?
options: Use parametric estimating
Use three-point estimating
Use bottom-up estimating
Use analogous estimating
questions.explanation: Estimate Costs develops an approximation of resource costs needed to complete project work, determining required monetary resources. Analogous estimating uses historical data from similar projects, applying parameters such as duration, budget, size or complexity as the basis for estimating costs for a future project. PMBOK Guide 8th Ed. Pages 62-63, 'Estimate Costs', and page 146, 'Analogous estimating'.

questions.text: Jordan is overseeing a consumer electronics project at ADH. After product release, several units are returned due to defects, requiring repairs and replacements. These unexpected expenses are impacting the project's profitability. Jordan wants to categorize these costs within quality-related expenses. Which type of cost is this?
options: Appraisal costs
Failure costs
Prevention costs
Conformance costs
questions.explanation: Failure costs are incurred when products fail to meet quality standards, including costs for rework, repairs, and returns, especially after delivery to customers. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Page 63, 'Estimate Costs' and pages 158-159, 'Cost of quality'.

questions.text: Lena is estimating costs for a bridge construction project at Arc Span Engineering. During planning, the team identifies potential soil instability that may require additional foundation work. The sponsor is concerned about handling this uncertainty without inflating the overall budget unnecessarily. What should Lena do?
options: Increase all activity cost estimates by 15% to account for the uncertainty
Add a management reserve
Include a contingency reserve
Update the cost baseline to include the additional foundation work
questions.explanation: Contingency reserves are set aside to implement a risk response or to respond to risk events should they occur. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Page 63, 'Estimate Costs' and page 266 'Contingency reserve'.

questions.text: Marcus, the project manager at SBI, is working with the team to finalize the project budget. Different cost estimates have already been developed for individual activities, and now Marcus needs a single approved version of the budget to track and measure future performance. What should Marcus do first?
options: Perform cost aggregation
Update the financial management plan
Finalize the cost baseline
Review the Business Case for the initial proposed cost
questions.explanation: Develop Budget aggregates estimated costs of activities or work packages to establish an authorized cost baseline for monitoring and control. Cost aggregation organizes estimates by work packages in the WBS and rolls them up to higher levels, such as control accounts, and ultimately the entire project. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Page 64, 'Develop Budget', and page 158, 'Cost aggregation'.

questions.text: Victor is managing a solar farm project at HEC. The team has completed cost estimates for individual work packages, but senior leaders in the organization question their accuracy and are concerned about project delivery. What will Victor do next?
options: Aggregate the estimates
Show them the basis of estimates
Show them the Financial Management Plan
Develop detailed cost estimates with the team
questions.explanation: Develop Budget is the process of aggregating estimated costs of activities or work packages to establish an authorized cost baseline, used to monitor and control performance. Inputs include the Basis of Estimates. PMBOK Guide, 8th Ed. Page 64, 'Develop Budget' and page 115, 'Basis of Estimates'.
